contain.definition: To hold something inside without letting it overflow or take control.
contain.example: Parents need to learn how to contain their child's feelings.

fundamental.definition: Basic and very important; at the core of something.
fundamental.example: Having your feelings understood is a fundamental human need.

prerequisite.definition: Something that must happen before something else can happen.
prerequisite.example: Feeling heard is a prerequisite for good mental health.

witnessed.example: Everyone needs their feelings to be witnessed.

significant.example: The significant people in your life shape who you become.

bundle.definition: A group of things held together; here, full of many things.
bundle.example: A baby is a bundle of feelings.

soothe.definition: To calm someone down and make them feel better.
soothe.example: Hold the baby and soothe them until they stop crying.

foundations.definition: The base that everything else is built on.
foundations.example: Early care builds the foundations for emotional health.

sensitively.definition: In a careful way that shows you understand someone's feelings.
sensitively.example: Responding sensitively to your child teaches them to trust.

rage.example: A child may feel rage when things do not go their way.

grief.definition: Deep sadness, usually because you lost something or someone.
grief.example: Grief is a natural feeling after a loss.

contentment.definition: A feeling of being happy and satisfied.
contentment.example: Contentment comes from feeling safe and loved.

generosity.definition: The quality of being willing to give and share.
generosity.example: Children learn generosity from watching their parents.

basis.definition: The starting point or reason for something.
basis.example: This is the basis of good mental health.

harmful.example: Denying feelings can be harmful to a child's health.

default.definition: What happens automatically if you do not choose something else.
default.example: Denying feelings is sometimes our default response.

belittle.definition: To make someone feel that they or their feelings are not important.
belittle.example: Do not belittle your child's fears.

scold.definition: To speak to someone angrily because they did something wrong.
scold.example: Do not scold a child for being upset.

unsettling.definition: Making you feel worried or uncomfortable.
unsettling.example: A child's anger can feel unsettling for parents.

disallowed.example: When feelings are disallowed, they go into hiding.

fester.definition: To get worse because it is not dealt with.
fester.example: Hidden feelings fester and cause trouble later.

vital.definition: Extremely important; necessary for life or success.
vital.example: It is vital to take your child's feelings seriously.

depression.definition: A serious mental condition where a person feels very sad for a long time.
depression.example: Not being soothed as a child can lead to depression.

comforted.definition: Made to feel better when sad or worried.
comforted.example: A child who is comforted learns to comfort themselves.

misattunements.definition: Times when a parent does not correctly respond to a child's feelings.
misattunements.example: Too many misattunements can hurt a child's emotional health.

tolerate.definition: To accept or deal with something difficult.
tolerate.example: Children need help learning to tolerate painful emotions.

diminishes.example: Their ability to handle hard feelings diminishes over time.

optimistic.definition: Expecting good things to happen; hopeful.
optimistic.example: Being soothed makes children more optimistic about their feelings.

susceptible.definition: Likely to be affected by something.
susceptible.example: Children who are soothed are less susceptible to anxiety.

anxiety.definition: A feeling of strong worry that does not go away.
anxiety.example: Anxiety can develop when feelings are not taken seriously.

instil.definition: To slowly put an idea or feeling into someone's mind.
instil.example: Good parenting instils a belief that feelings will pass.

acceptable.definition: Good enough; able to be accepted.
acceptable.example: Whatever you feel, you are still acceptable.

habitual.example: The new way of responding can become habitual.

alien.example: It may feel alien at first to accept your child's anger.

repressing.definition: Pushing feelings down and hiding them.
repressing.example: Repressing feelings does not make them go away.

repressor.definition: A person who pushes away or hides their feelings.
repressor.example: A repressor might say 'Don't make a fuss.'

inclination.definition: A natural tendency to do something.
inclination.example: Her natural inclination was to push away strong feelings.

confronted.definition: Faced with something difficult or challenging.
confronted.example: When confronted with tears, she said 'Be brave.'

fuss.definition: Unnecessary worry or excitement about something small.
fuss.example: Don't make a fuss — nothing is wrong.

dismiss.definition: To treat something as not important.
dismiss.example: If you dismiss a child's feeling, they will stop sharing.

subsequent.example: The child will hide subsequent feelings from you.

overreacting.definition: Responding with too much emotion; more than is needed.
overreacting.example: Overreacting to your child's tears can make things worse.

hysterical.definition: Extremely emotional and unable to control yourself.
hysterical.example: She became as hysterical as her crying child.

nursery.definition: A place where young children are cared for during the day.
nursery.example: Dropping your child off at nursery can be hard.

invade.definition: To enter somewhere without being wanted; to push into someone's space.
invade.example: They may feel you invade them by taking over their feelings.

merging.example: Merging with your child's feelings is not helpful.

containing.definition: Holding feelings in a way that is calm and accepting.
containing.example: Containing means taking feelings seriously without overreacting.

cuddle.example: Would you like a cuddle to feel better?

container.definition: Something that holds things; here, a person who can hold another's feelings.
container.example: A parent should be a container for their child's emotions.

overwhelmed.definition: Feeling like something is too much to handle.
overwhelmed.example: Parents should not be overwhelmed by their child's anger.

psychotherapists.definition: People trained to help others with mental and emotional problems through talking.
psychotherapists.example: Psychotherapists help their clients process difficult feelings.

clients.definition: People who pay for a professional service.
clients.example: The therapist works with many different clients.

witnessing.definition: Seeing and being present for something happening.
witnessing.example: Witnessing anger means staying calm while the child is upset.

punitive.example: Do not be punitive when your child is angry.

associations.definition: Connections made in your mind between things.
associations.example: Loud voices may have associations of love for some people.

inured.definition: Used to something unpleasant so it no longer bothers you.
inured.example: Growing up with shouting made her inured to raised voices.

confrontation.definition: A direct fight or argument with someone.

manipulated.example: If you felt manipulated as a child, you may distrust love.

distrust.example: Past hurt can cause you to distrust warmth.

sting.definition: A sudden sharp pain; here, a hidden hurt.
sting.example: She expected love to be accompanied by a sting.

inconvenient.definition: Causing trouble or difficulty at a bad time.
inconvenient.example: Even inconvenient emotions are important signals.

dashboard.definition: The panel in a car with controls and warning lights.
dashboard.example: Think of emotions as warning lights on a dashboard.

petrol.definition: The fuel used to power a car; gasoline.
petrol.example: The petrol warning light means the car needs fuel.

bulb.definition: The glass part of a light that produces light.
bulb.example: Removing the bulb does not fix the problem.

deaden.definition: To make something less strong or less felt.
deaden.example: We should not try to deaden our feelings.

heed.definition: To pay attention to and follow advice or a warning.
heed.example: Heed your feelings — they are telling you something important.
